bio.biography:
Quick Facts

  • Specialty: Ice Dance

  • Daughter of Susan and Brad Hubbell

  • She has two older brothers, Keiffer and Zachary

  • Began skating at age five

  • Previously skated with her older brother, Keiffer, before partnering with Zachary Donohue for over a decade, becoming one of the top ice dance teams in the world

  • Retired from competitive skating in 2022 and transitioned to coaching and choreography

  • Enjoys baking, scrapbooking and making jewelry

Biographical Information

Madison Hubbell is a former American ice dancer and two-time Olympian. Born in Lansing, Michigan, she began skating at age five and initially competed in ice dance with her older brother, Keiffer. She later partnered with Zachary Donohue in 2011, forming a team that became one of the most successful in U.S. ice dance history.

Hubbell and Donohue competed at two Olympic Games, winning silver in the team event and bronze in ice dance at the Olympic Winter Games Beijing 2022. They were also three-time U.S. national champions and earned four world championship medals. Their emotional and dynamic performances made them fan favorites throughout their career.

After retiring from competition in 2022, Hubbell transitioned into coaching and choreography, using her experience to mentor the next generation of skaters. Off the ice, she enjoys yoga and working with young athletes to develop their artistry and technical skills. In 2020, she hosted a virtual yoga class and donated the proceeds to the Memorial Fund, advocating for same-sex partnerships in competitive figure skating. 

Olympic Experience
  • 2-time Olympian; 2-time Olympic medalist (1 gold, 1 bronze)
    • Olympic Games Beijing 2022, gold (Team - Mixed), bronze (Ice Dance - Mixed)
    • Olympic Games Pyeongchang 2018, 4th (Ice Dance - Mixed)
World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2022 – silver (Ice Dance - Mixed)
  • Years of participation: Ice Dance - Mixed 2012, 2015, 2016, 2017, 2018, 2019, 2021, 2022
  • Medals: 4 (3 silver, 1 bronze)
    • Silver – 2022 (Ice Dance - Mixed); 2021 (Ice Dance - Mixed); 2018 (Ice Dance - Mixed)
    • Bronze – 2019 (Ice Dance - Mixed)

  • 2011 USA Cycling Cross-Country Mountain Bike National Championships, 6th (short track cross-country), 9th (cross country)

    • Olympic Games Tokyo 2020, 31th (Cross Country - Women)

  • Years of participation: Cross Country - Women 2012, 2014, 2015, 2016, 2017, 2018, 2019, 2020; Cross Country Team Relay - Mixed 2020

bio.fun_fact: Cofounded "Culxtured," a media collective spotlighting para athletes' stories and brands
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Speciality: Snowboard Cross and Banked Slalom
  • Daughter of Jeffry and Kristie Huckaby
  • Has two brothers, Jeremy and Jordan
  • Married to Tristan Clegg, a former professional snowboard cross athlete
  • Has two daughters, Lilah and Sloan
  • Hobbies include mountain biking, sewing and gardening
  • Cofounded "Culxtured," a media collective spotlighting Para athletes' stories and brands
Biographical Information

Brenna Huckaby grew up in Louisiana as a competitive gymnast, but after dealing with lingering knee pain, she was diagnosed with osteosarcoma, a rare bone cancer, and had her right leg amputated at age 14. Shortly after, Huckaby was first introduced to snowboarding on a rehabilitation trip to Utah.

Huckaby made her Paralympic debut in 2018 and won double gold at the Paralympic Winter Games PyeongChang 2018 in snowboard cross and banked slalom. Before the Winter Paralympic Games Beijing 2022, the IPC cut the events she won in PyeongChang in women's Para snowboarding opting to focus on an event geared towards less impaired athletes.

A leading voice in disability advocacy, Huckaby orchestrated a multi-faceted inclusivity campaign in the lead-up to the Beijing 2022, for which she earned the right to compete in medal events initially limited to less impaired athletes. Huckaby went on to win gold and bronze at the Beijing Games and continues to advocate for increased disability representation and inclusion in all areas of society. 

Off the snow, she loves spending time with her husband and two daughters. They snowboard in the winter and mountain bike in the summer together as a family.

Paralympic Experience
  • 3-time Paralympian; 5-time Paralympic medalist (3 gold, 2 bronze)
    • Paralympic Games Milan 2026, bronze (Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 2 Impaired - Women), 6th (Snowboard Cross - Lower Limb 2 Impaired - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Beijing 2022, gold (Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 2 Impaired - Women), bronze (Snowboard Cross - Lower Limb 2 Impaired - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Pyeongchang 2018, gold (Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Women, Snowboard Cross - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Women)
World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2025 – bronze (Snowboard Cross - Lower Limb 2 Impaired - Women), 5th (Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 2 Impaired - Women, Snowboard Cross Team Event - Mixed)
  • Years of participation: Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 2 Impaired - Women 2025; Snowboard Cross - Lower Limb 2 Impaired - Women 2025; Snowboard Cross Team Event - Mixed 2025; Dual Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Women 2023; Dual Banked Slalom - Team - Women 2023; Snowboard Cross - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Women 2015, 2017, 2022, 2023; Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Women 2015, 2017, 2022
  • Medals: 10 (5 gold, 3 silver, 2 bronze)
    • Gold – 2023 (Dual Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Women); 2022 (Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Women); 2017 (Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Women, Snowboard Cross - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Women); 2015 (Snowboard Cross - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Women)
    • Silver – 2023 (Snowboard Cross - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Women); 2022 (Snowboard Cross - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Women); 2015 (Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Women)
    • Bronze – 2025 (Snowboard Cross - Lower Limb 2 Impaired - Women); 2023 (Dual Banked Slalom - Team - Women)

bio.education: Buchholz High School, Santa Fe College
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • I started karate at age 6 at an after school facility that had many different options for sports and activities. I was supposed to be taking a dance class but ended up in the wrong line on the first day and went to karate class instead. I’ve done karate ever since
  • I started karate when I was 6 years old and have been competing ever since
  • I first made the Jr. national team at age 14 and have been competing for the US team since then
  • I trained for fifteen years in Gainesville, Florida under Sensei Jason Sorgi. Then I moved to South Carolina for a job opportunity with Greenville County EMS as a paramedic and to train under Sensei Tommy Hood
  • I met my husband while we both worked as paramedics with Greenville County EMS and we will be married five years in September.
  • We have both an adopted son and a biological son, both of whom come with me to almost every single training and most tournaments
  • Three things people may not know about me is that I have competed in over twenty USA national championships since I started karate, I dabbled in pole vaulting, discus and weightlifting while in high school, I met my husband while we were both working as paramedics for Greenville County EMS.
Career Highlights
  • Pan American Games, Toronto, 2015 (-50kg Kumite - Women)
  • Pan American Games, Guadalajara, 2011 (-50kg Kumite - Women)
  • World Junior & Cadet Championships, Rabat, 2009 (-48kg Kumite (Junior) - Women)
World Championship Experience
  • Most recent: 2021 – 20th (-50kg Kumite - Women)
  • Years of participation: -50kg Kumite - Women 2010, 2012, 2021
  • Medals: 1 (1 bronze)
    • Bronze – 2010 (-50kg Kumite - Women)

bio.education: Eustis Farnam Public Schools (Eustis, Neb.) ‘04 Southeast Community College ‘09, Livestock Management
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Kevan Hueftle was a successful high school athlete before being involved in a hunting accident in 2005 that left his foot severely injured from a gunshot wound. After three surgeries and five months of healing, he made the decision to amputate his left leg below the knee. He struggled with alcohol addiction and several other setbacks in his recovery. Still the holder of the state high school record in the 4x400-meter relay, he was determined to be active again and live a healthy lifestyle, pursuing Paralympic track and field in 2017 and making his first international team at the 2019 Parapan American Games
  • Son of Lee and Sherry Hueftle
  • Has two sisters, Kara and Kristine
  • Married to Nicole
  • Has a daughter, Chesley, and son, Hayes
  • Oversees Kevan Farms and Ranches with his parents, raising red angus that he sells directly to his consumers.
Career Highlights
  • ParaPan American Games, Lima, 2019 (100m T64 - Men, 200m T64 - Men)
World Championship Experience

  • Years of participation: 100m T64 - Men 2019; 200m T64 - Men 2019
  • Top finish: 4th – 2019 (100m T64 - Men), 2019 (200m T64 - Men)

Career Highlights

Quick Facts

Edison High School (Huntington Beach, Calif.) '03 University of California, Los Angeles

Quick Facts
  • Position: Second
  • Speciality: Men's Curling
  • Grew up in Fairbanks, Alaska as part of a family that has played the sport of curling for generations
  • Two-time national champion (2016, 2018)
  • Mechanical engineer
Biographical Information 

Colin Hufman grew up in Fairbanks, Alaska as part of a family that has played the sport of curling for generations.  Colin realized his own potential when he was the runner-up at age 10 in a local cash prize hotshot curling tournament, where individuals twice his age squared off against him in an individual shooting skill competition.

In 2002, Hufman would be part of the team that won the U.S. junior curling championships. He made his Olympic debut, twenty years later, at the Olympic Winter Games Beijing 2022 as the alternate for the U.S. men's team, the defending gold medalists. Hufman filled in for lead John Landsteiner in a round-robin loss during the Beijing Games and replaced Landsteiner full-time as the fourth member of Team Shuster when Landsteiner retired at the end of the 2024-25 curling season. Before joining Team Shuster, Hufman was part of two national championship teams in 2016 and 2018. 

Off the ice, Hufman got his degree in mechanical engineering from the University of Washington Bothell in 2016. Hufman thinks mechanical engineering and curling are very related: "The ice conditions are always different, and so is the way you throw the rock, figuring how to adapt based on the varying conditions requires similar problem-solving skills to that of mechanical engineering."

Olympic Experience
  • 1-time Olympian
    • Olympic Games Beijing 2022, 4th (Team - Men)
World Championship Experience
  • Most recent: 2024 – 6th (Team - Men)
  • Years of participation: Team - Men 2018, 2021, 2023, 2024
  • Top finish: 5th – 2021 (Team - Men)

bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Position - Defenseman
  • Father Jim Hughes served as team captain at Providence College, worked as an assistant coach for the Boston Bruins, and was Director of Player Development for the Toronto Maple Leafs 
  • Mother Ellen Weinberg-Hughes was a standout athlete at the University of New Hampshire, she played hockey, lacrosse, and soccer, represented the U.S. women’s national ice hockey team 
  • Has two brothers, Jack and Luke, who also play in the NHL
Biographical Information

Quinn Hughes played college hockey at the University of Michigan from 2017 to 2019, where he studied Sport Management and earned All-American honors. Hughes was drafted seventh overall by Vancouver in the 2018 NHL Draft and made his debut in 2019. Hughes was traded to the Minnesota Wild in December 2025 after spending 7.5 seasons with the Canucks.

    • Olympic Games Milan 2026, gold (Team - Men)

  • Years of participation: Team - Men 2018, 2019
  • Medals: 1 (1 bronze)
    • Bronze – 2018 (Team - Men)
